worth_it_explanation: Access governance decides who can change the configuration that earns a publisher money, so a stale admin account or a shared API token is a standing risk to both revenue and data. The check is a review of the user list and the credentials a publisher already holds in Equativ, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete account or token the publisher can scope down, rotate, or remove. A departed employee who still holds console access, or an API token shared across several integrations, is the kind of gap that stays invisible until it causes a problem.
intro: Access governance in the Equativ publisher console controls who can read and change the setup that runs a publisher's monetization, from pricing and inventory to the integrations that connect demand. This subcategory is about the people and the machine credentials that hold that access, the roles assigned to users and the scope and freshness of the API tokens, rather than the configuration those accounts touch. These checks confirm that access follows the principle of least privilege, that departed staff no longer hold a login, and that API credentials are scoped to what each integration needs and rotated rather than shared and left in place.

audit_checks.title: User roles do not follow least privilege and departed staff retain access
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Every user who can change pricing, inventory, or demand connections in the Equativ console is a path to a costly misconfiguration or a data exposure. When admin rights are handed out broadly or a departed employee keeps a login, the publisher carries more risk than the work requires, and a single mistaken or unauthorized change can move revenue or leak account data.

educate.base:
The Equativ publisher console gives different users different powers, from reading reports to changing pricing, editing inventory, and connecting demand partners. The principle of least privilege says each user should hold only the access the job needs and no more. A reporting analyst does not need rights to edit pricing rules, and a contractor brought in for one project does not need a standing admin login. The more accounts that can change revenue-bearing configuration, the larger the surface for a mistaken change, and the harder it is to trace who changed what when something moves.

The risk grows with two patterns. The first is broad admin access, where several users hold full rights because granting admin was faster than assigning a narrower role. The second is access that outlives the person, where an employee leaves or a contractor finishes but the login stays active. A dormant admin account is both a path for an unauthorized change and a credential that can be taken over without anyone noticing because no one is watching an account no one is using.

Equativ supports assigning users roles that scope what each can see and do, so a publisher can match access to responsibility rather than giving everyone the same broad rights. The audit question is whether the current user list reflects that, with each active user holding a role that fits the job, and with no login left active for someone who has moved on.

A few signs point to access that does not follow least privilege:
  • Several users hold full admin rights when their work only requires reporting or a single area of the console
  • Active logins exist for employees or contractors who have left, with no record of when access was last reviewed
  • Roles were assigned once at onboarding and never revisited as responsibilities changed
You may want to review the full user list against current responsibilities, confirm each active account still belongs to someone who needs it, and scope each role to the work the person actually does. One approach is to set a recurring access review so departures and role changes are caught on a schedule rather than by chance.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media organization, console access often spreads across an ad operations team, finance, and outside contractors brought in for specific campaigns or migrations. You may want to confirm that contractor and freelancer logins are removed when the engagement ends, and that finance or editorial users who only read reports hold a reporting role rather than rights to change pricing or inventory.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content business, the team monetizing inventory may overlap with staff focused on the storefront, and access can be granted in a hurry during a launch. You may want to confirm that anyone given console access for a one-time setup still needs it, and that roles separate the people who change monetization configuration from those who only need to see how it performs.

learn_more.source_title: Authorization Cheat Sheet (least privilege and periodic permission review)

educate.learn_more_note: Equativ's detailed documentation for managing users and assigning roles in the publisher console is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the full list of users with access to the Equativ console, the role each holds, and whether each active account still belongs to someone whose job requires that level of access.

equativ_console.label: Equativ publisher console
steps: Open the user management area of the console and export or list every user with access, along with the role assigned to each.
For each user, record whether they are still with the organization and whether the role assigned matches their current responsibilities.
Flag any account holding full admin rights whose work only requires reporting or a single area of the console.
Flag any active login belonging to an employee or contractor who has left, and ask the publisher when access was last reviewed.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the full user list with each user's role and current status. A setup where every active account belongs to someone who needs it, each role fits the job, and access is reviewed on a recurring schedule passes. A setup with broad admin access that exceeds the work, active logins for departed staff, or no record of any access review is a fail recorded with the accounts and roles affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the user list, each assigned role, and each user's current status above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that access to the Equativ console at {{site_name}} does not follow the principle of least privilege, with broad admin rights and active logins for people who no longer need them. Every account that can change pricing, inventory, or demand connections is a path to a costly change or a data exposure, so access that exceeds the work or outlives the person carries risk the publisher does not need to hold. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the full user list against current responsibilities, scoping each role to the work the person actually does, removing logins for departed staff, and setting a recurring access review so departures and role changes are caught on a schedule. After the cleanup, keep the review on a fixed cadence so access stays matched to need.

audit_checks.title: API credentials are shared and not scoped or rotated
audit_checks.impact_rationale: An Equativ API credential can read and change account configuration programmatically, so a token that is over-scoped, shared across integrations, or never rotated is a high-value target that stays usable indefinitely. A leaked or stolen credential that grants more than one integration needs gives an attacker broad reach, and a shared token makes it hard to tell which integration leaked it or to revoke one without breaking the rest.

educate.base:
Equativ exposes an Application Programming Interface (API) so a publisher can read reports and change configuration from outside the console, which is how data warehouses, yield tools, and custom dashboards connect. Each connection authenticates with a credential, such as an API key or an access token. The same security principles that govern human logins apply to these machine credentials, with two that matter most here. A credential should be scoped to only the access the integration needs, and it should be rotated on a schedule rather than created once and left in place forever.

Scope is the first concern. A reporting integration that only reads data does not need a credential that can also change pricing or inventory. When one broad token is reused for every integration, a leak of that single token exposes everything it can reach, and there is no way to grant a read-only tool read-only access. Sharing one credential across several integrations also makes a leak harder to trace because any of the connections could be the source, and revoking the token to contain the leak breaks all of them at once.

Rotation is the second concern. A credential that never changes will keep working for whoever holds it, including anyone who copies it out of a config file, a shared document, or a former employee's notes. Rotating credentials on a schedule limits how long a stolen one stays useful, and it forces the publisher to keep an accurate record of which integrations are still live. A credential no rotation ever touches is often a credential no one remembers exists.

A few signs point to API credentials that are not scoped or rotated:
  • One API credential is reused across several integrations rather than each integration holding its own
  • A read-only tool authenticates with a credential that can also change configuration
  • No credential has been rotated since it was first created, and no rotation schedule exists
  • Credentials are stored in shared documents or config files that more people can reach than need them
You may want to confirm that each integration holds its own credential, scoped to the access it needs, and that credentials are rotated on a schedule and stored where only the integration can reach them. One approach is to inventory every active credential, retire any that no live integration uses, and set a rotation cadence going forward.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media organization, reporting and yield data often flow into a data warehouse or a business-intelligence tool through an API connection that an engineering team set up once. You may want to confirm that the credential behind that pipeline is read-only if the tool only reads data, and that it is rotated on a schedule rather than left as the one-time key from the original build.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content business, monetization data may feed the same systems that handle storefront and customer data, which raises the stakes on a leaked credential. You may want to confirm that the Equativ API credential is scoped narrowly, kept separate from credentials for other systems, and rotated on a schedule so a single stale key cannot bridge monetization and commerce data.

learn_more.source_title: Secrets Management Cheat Sheet (rotation, least privilege, avoiding shared secrets)

educate.learn_more_note: Equativ's documentation for creating and managing API credentials and access tokens is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review every active API credential connecting to the Equativ account, the access each one grants, the integrations that use it, and when each was last rotated.

equativ_console.label: Equativ publisher console
steps: Open the API or access-token area of the console and list every active credential along with the scope or permissions each grants.
For each credential, record which integration uses it and whether that integration needs the full access the credential allows.
Flag any credential reused across more than one integration, and any read-only integration authenticating with a credential that can change configuration.
Ask the publisher when each credential was last rotated and whether a rotation schedule exists, and note any credential no live integration still uses.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records every active credential with its scope, the integration that uses it, and its last rotation date. A setup where each integration holds its own credential scoped to what it needs, credentials are rotated on a schedule, and no unused credential remains active passes. A setup with credentials shared across integrations, scope that exceeds what the integration needs, or credentials never rotated and never reviewed is a fail recorded with the credentials and integrations affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each active credential, its scope, the integration using it, and its last rotation date above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the Equativ API credentials at {{site_name}} are shared across integrations and are neither scoped to least privilege nor rotated on a schedule. An API credential can read and change account configuration programmatically, so a credential that grants more than an integration needs, is reused across several connections, or never changes is a high-value target that stays usable indefinitely and is hard to trace when it leaks. {{voice_recommend}} giving each integration its own credential scoped to only the access it needs, retiring any credential no live integration uses, and setting a rotation schedule so a stolen credential stops working before long. After the cleanup, store each credential where only its integration can reach it and keep rotation on a fixed cadence.
